coredata.dc:title: In-situ synthesis of carbon dots embedded wrinkled-mesoporous silica microspheres for efficiently capturing and monitoring organochlorine pesticides from water and fruit juice
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Shengrui
Liu, Hailin
Long, Anying
Feng, Suling
Chen, Chang-Po
coredata.dc:description: An in-situ synthesis strategy was proposed for fabrication of carbon dots embedded wrinkled mesoporous silica (CDs/WMS) microspheres. Characterizations results demonstrated that the doping of CDs with a moderate content still allowed the microspheres to maintain wrinkled and highly mesoporous structures. The CDs/WMS composite was subsequently employed as solid phase microextraction (SPME) coating for sensitive determination of ultra-trace organochlorine pesticides (OCPs) from aqueous solutions. High surface area, abundant chemical groups, and plentiful porosity made CDs/WMS microspheres present superior extraction capacities for OCPs compared with commercial SPME coatings and pure WMS. Under the optimum extraction conditions, the proposed analytical method exhibited wide linearity range (0.2–1000 ng/L), low detection of limits (0.0023–0.073 ng/L for water, 0.0062–0.41 ng/L for fruit juice), and satisfactory reproducibility with relative standard deviations of 6.2 %−13.3 % and 7.3 %−13.2 % for water and fruit juice, respectively. Finally, the proposed method was successfully used for determination of OCPs in real water and fruit juice samples. The recoveries varied from 80.1 % to 105.2 % for water and 71 % to 106.8 % for fruit juice. This study developed a novel strategy for synthesis of CDs/WMS microspheres, and proposed a new type of SPME coating, which expanded the potential of CDs in capturing organic pollutants from aqueous solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Wrinkled mesoporous silica microsphere
Solid phase microextraction
Organochlorine pesticides

Characterizations and formation machanisms of CDs/WMS microspheres
SPME performances and mechanisms of CDs/WMS coating
Method performances for determining OCPs from water and fruit juice
Analysis of OCPs from real water and fruit juice samples

We also would like to thank Gang Li from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the BET analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a, b) pure WMS, (c) CDs/WMS-1, (d) CDs/WMS-2, and (e) CDs/WMS-3; (f, g) TEM images of CDs/WMS-2 and (h) elements mapping results of O, Si, C, and N for CDs/WMS-2.
Synthesis mechanisms of pure WMS and CDs/WMS microspheres.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) XPS survey spectra, and high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) Si 2p, (d) O 1s, (e) C 1s and (f) N 1s for WMS and CDs/WMS.
(a) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and (b) pore size distributions of WMS, CDs/WMS-2, and CDs/WMS-3; (c) TG curves of WMS and CDs/WMS-2 under nitrogen atmosphere.
(a) Extraction efficiencies of WMS and CDs/WMS composites with different doping amounts of CDs; (b) GC–MS chromatograms of OCPs by CDs/WMS coating and commercial SPME coatings.
Schematic diagram of the potential extraction mechanism of CDs/WMS microspheres.
Analytical performance of CDs/WMS SPME coating towards OCPs.
Comparisons with other methods for SPME of OCPs from water.
Analytical results and recoveries for determination of OCPs from real water samples.
Analytical results and recoveries for determination of OCPs from fruit juice samples.

head.ce:title: In-situ synthesis of carbon dots embedded wrinkled-mesoporous silica microspheres for efficiently capturing and monitoring organochlorine pesticides from water and fruit juice

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions, Ministry of Education, Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province for Green Manufacturing of Fine Chemicals, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang 453007, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions
Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province for Green Manufacturing of Fine Chemicals
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions, Ministry of Education, Collaborative Innovation Center of Henan Province for Green Manufacturing of Fine Chemicals, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang 453007, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: 113 Geological Brigade, Guizhou Bureau of Geology and Mineral Resources, Liupanshui 553000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guizhou Bureau of Geology and Mineral Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 113 Geological Brigade, Guizhou Bureau of Geology and Mineral Resources, Liupanshui 553000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An in-situ synthesis strategy was proposed for fabrication of carbon dots embedded wrinkled mesoporous silica (CDs/WMS) microspheres. Characterizations results demonstrated that the doping of CDs with a moderate content still allowed the microspheres to maintain wrinkled and highly mesoporous structures. The CDs/WMS composite was subsequently employed as solid phase microextraction (SPME) coating for sensitive determination of ultra-trace organochlorine pesticides (OCPs) from aqueous solutions. High surface area, abundant chemical groups, and plentiful porosity made CDs/WMS microspheres present superior extraction capacities for OCPs compared with commercial SPME coatings and pure WMS. Under the optimum extraction conditions, the proposed analytical method exhibited wide linearity range (0.2–1000 ng/L), low detection of limits (0.0023–0.073 ng/L for water, 0.0062–0.41 ng/L for fruit juice), and satisfactory reproducibility with relative standard deviations of 6.2 %−13.3 % and 7.3 %−13.2 % for water and fruit juice, respectively. Finally, the proposed method was successfully used for determination of OCPs in real water and fruit juice samples. The recoveries varied from 80.1 % to 105.2 % for water and 71 % to 106.8 % for fruit juice. This study developed a novel strategy for synthesis of CDs/WMS microspheres, and proposed a new type of SPME coating, which expanded the potential of CDs in capturing organic pollutants from aqueous solutions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In past decades, organochlorine pesticides (OCPs) have been widely used in agricultural production to control the pests and diseases. However, the residues of OCPs in crops, fruits, and water cause serious damage to human health due to their strong toxicity and low degradability [1]. In view of harmfulness to ecosystem and environment, OCPs have been listed as the priority pollutants and were banned in many countries. Notably, trace OCPs residues are still found in various water and fruit juice samples [2,3]. Therefore, it is of great significance to establish a sensitive method to analyze the ultra-trace OCPs in water and fruit juice samples.

In general, the determination of OCPs was implemented by sample pretreatment prior to gas chromatography or gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) to increase the sensitivity and diminish the effect of sample marix [4,5]. Although various pretreatment techniques such as liquid–liquid extraction and solid extraction have been widely applied in analysis of OCPs, the consumption of organic solvent and tedious extraction procedures cannot meet the requirements of green and simple analysis [6–12]. Since introduced by Pawliszyn and his coworker in 1990, solid–phase microextraction (SPME) has attracted much attention by analysts owing to the advantages such as high sensitivity, solvent-free approach, and simple operation, etc. [13]. The SPME technique exhibited significant potential in food, environmental, pharmaceutical, and biological analysis during the past years [14–18]. The coating materials play a vital role in extraction efficiency for organic pollutants. Although various commercial SPME coatings based on polymers have been developed, a series of emerging nanomaterials including metal oxides, carbon-based nanomaterials, metal–organic frameworks, and covalent organic frameworks, etc. were proposed as SPME coatings in order to improve the extraction efficiency [19–21].

Among the nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) is a type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, and has been extensively applied in chemical sensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, catalysis, etc. because of its unique physicochemical properties [22,23]. Moreover, CDs also exhibits significant potential in capturing pollutant from environmental samples owing to its abundant chemical groups, environmental friendliness, simple preparation, and low cost [24]. However, CDs is difficult to be separated from aqueous solution after the adsorption of targeted compounds due to its strong hydrophily. Therefore, to address this challenge, a supported material is required for the immobilization of CDs in practical applications [25–28]. In our previous study, CDs was innovatively used as SPME coating for sensitive determination of chlorobenzenes in water by using graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) as the supported material. Results showed that the CDs/g-C3N4 composite exhibited superior extraction efficiencies for chlorobenzenes compared with pure g-C3N4 [29]. In order to further explore the application of CDs and observe more efficient extraction capacity for targeted compounds, an alternative support material need to be developed.

It is well known that mesoporous silicas present excellent adsorption capacity for pollutants owing to its outstanding advantages of large surface area, high porosities, and excellent thermal and chemical stability [30–32]. In addition, chemical tenability allows mesoporous silicas to be decorated straightforwardly as the supported material [33,34]. It is worth noting that the adsorption capacity for targeted compounds depends on the porous structures, which allow the targeted compounds transfer into the inner surface, and provide more abundant active sites. Polshettiwar et al. [35] first fabricated a type of high-surface-area mesoporous silica microspheres with fibrous morphologies, which provided abundant channels for molecules transport. The as-prepared mesoporous silica microspheres presented remarkable prospect as supported material, and was named as wrinkled mesoporous silicas (WMS) subsequently [36–39]. The preparation of doped mesoporous silicas can be implemented by two-steps deposition or in-situ strategy. Wherein, synthesis of nanoparticles doped mesoporous silicas by in-situ strategy effectively avoided pore blocking and uneven distribution of nanoparticles caused by two-steps deposition method [40,41]. Moreover, the synthesis procedures were simplified to be one step. Therefore, in-situ synthesis strategy attracted great interest for preparation of doped mesoporous silicas.

In this study, we innovatively synthesized a type of CDs embedded WMS (CDs/WMS) microspheres by in-situ strategy, and evaluated its extraction performances for determining ultra-trace OCPs from water and fruit juice samples by means of SPME coupled with GC–MS (Scheme 1 ). Wherein, CDs was assembled into silica precursor in the presence of micelle template, and formed CDs/WMS microspheres following with crystallization of silica and removal of template. The comparisons of CDs/WMS microspheres as SPME coating for extraction and determination of ultra-trace OCPs with commercial coatings and reported methods were investigated. Moreover, the mechanisms for formation of CDs/WMS microspheres and enhanced extraction performances were also explored. Finally, the proposed analytical method based on CDs/WMS SPME coating was applied to determine OCPs from real water and fruit juice samples successfully.

The OCPs standards including propachlor (PPC), trifluralin (TFR), hexachlorobenzene (HCB), chlorpyrifos (CPF), g-chlordance (g-CHD), a-chlordane (a-CHD), trans-nonachlor (trans-NC), and chlorobenzilate (CBZ) were purchased from AccuStandard (New Haven, CT, USA). Ethylenediamine, citric acid, ethyl silicate (TEOS), and ethanol were obtained from Sinopharm Group Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), cyclohexane, urea, and n-butanol were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Stainless-steel wire (O.D. 100 μm) was obtained from Shenzhen Bao Fang Metal Materials (Guangdong, China). Sylard 184 silicone elastomer was purchased from Dow Silicone Co. (USA). Commercial SPME coatings including PA (85 μm), DVB/CAR/PDMS (30 μm), and PDMS (100 μm) were provided by ANPEL Laboratory Technologies Inc. (Shanghai, China). The instruments, which were used for materials characterizations and detection of OCPs, were described in Section S1.

Synthesis of CDs. The preparation of CDs was carried out using citric acid and ethylenediamine as the precursor with hydrothermal treatment according to literature with modifications [42]. Briefly, a mixture consisted of 6 g, 2 mL ethylenediamine, and 50 mL deionized water was placed into a 100-mL Teflon-lined autoclave, and reaction at 180 °C for 6 h in an oven. After that, the obtained dark brown liquid was filtered and dialyzed for 48 h. The CDs powder was finally observed after drying in a vacuum oven at 80 °C.

Preparation of CDs/WMS microspheres. For the preparation of CDs/WMS microspheres, in-situ assembly strategy was implemented during the formation of WMS microspheres. Typically, 1 g CTAB and 2 g urea were added into mixed solution consisted of CDs solution (15 mL) and n-butanol (1.2 mL) under stirring at room temperature. Then, cyclohexane (15 mL) were added into the solution, and stirred for 30 min. After that, 1.5 mL TEOS was added to the mixture, and stirred for another 30 min. After reaction for 24 h at 70 °C, the obtained precipitate was collected by centrifugation and washed with ethanol and deionized water for three times, respectively. The resulting powder was then treated in in mixed solution containing ethanol (50 mL) and HCl (5 mL) for 24 h at 70 °C to remove the CTAB templates. Finally, the CDs/WMS microspheres were observed after centrifugation, washing by ethanol and deionized water, and drying at at 80 °C. To change the embedded amounts of CDs, the concentration of CDs were set at 1, 2, and 5 mg mL−1, which were labeled as CDs/WMS-1, CDs/WMS-2, and CDs/WMS-3, respectively. For the preparation of pure WMS microspheres, CDs solution was replaced with deionized water according to the preparation procedures above.

The CDs/WMS SPME coating was fabricated by an adhesion treatment using silicone sealant according to previous reported methods. A stainless-steel wire (O.D. ∼ 100 μm) was cut into 3–4 cm in length, and washed by acetone, hydrochloric acid (1.2 mol/L), and deionized water, respectively. The cleaned stainless-steel wire was immersed into silicone sealant solution first, and then rotated on the CDs/WMS powder to form a uniform coating. After that, the coated stainless-steel wire was dried in an over at 120 °C. The final SPME fiber was obtained by assembling coated stainless-steel wire into an empty SPME needle. Before used for extraction, the as-prepared CDs/WMS coating was aged for 20 min at 260 °C in the GC–MS injector.

The SPME procedures were implemented with immersion mode by using 20–mL vials containing 10–mL solutions. The sample vial was placed on a heater first, and then the SPME fiber was inserted into the sample solution for extraction. After extraction, the SPME fiber was withdrawn from sample vial, and introduced into GC–MS injector for desorption and analysis. Therein, extraction temperature was controlled in the range of 30–60 °C, extraction time varied from 10 to 40 min. Diluted hydrochloric acid (0.24 mol/L) and sodium hydroxide solution (0.24 mol/L) were used to adjusted the solution acidity with pH values from 3 to 9. Sodium chloride solution with contents of 0–30 % was used to change the solution ionic strength. The operation parameters of GC–MS and characteristic ions of eight OCPs were detailed in the supplementary information (Section S2, Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection and analysis of real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The water samples were collected from a nameless lake (1#, 2#) located in Puding County, Guizhou Province, and campus tap water (3#). The fruit juice samples including cranberry juice, grape juice, and peach juice were obtained from the local supermarket. For the analysis of real samples, the collected water (10 mL) was placed into sample vial for SPME and detection by GC–MS without any other treatment. To avoid the effect of sample matrix, the fruit juice was treated by centrifugation at 4000 rpm to remove the precipitates, and then diluted three times with deionized water. Subsequently, the pretreated fruit juice samples were analyzed by the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations and formation machanisms of CDs/WMS microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the micro morphologies of the as-prepared materials, SEM and TEM were used to characterize the WMS and CDs/WMS microspheres. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, the as-prepared WMS exhibit uniform monodisperse microspheres with an average diameter of ∼528 nm. The WMS microspheres possess abundant pores consisted of wrinkled silica walls with thickness of ∼16.1 nm. After the introduction of CDs, the CDs/WMS maintains the spherical structures with CDs concentration of 1 and 2 mg mL−1 (Fig. 1c and d). However, the diameters of microspheres decrease to ∼279 nm. Moreover, the thickness of wrinkled silica walls increase from ∼16.1 to ∼18.7 nm, and the pores dimensions become larger. Further increase of CDs concentration (5 mg mL−1) leads to severe agglomeration, and the spherical structures of CDs/WMS are destroyed (Fig. 1e). The generated pores on the WMS and CDs/WMS suggested sufficient channels for compounds transferring from outside to inner surface, thus enhanced the active sites for adsorption. TEM image (Fig. 1f and g) further demonstrates the formation of radial morphology consisted by wrinkled silica sheets, which leads to numerous interspaces among wrinkled silica sheets. Elements mapping analysis (Fig. 1h) show that the elements of C, N, O, and Si are distributed on the CDs/WMS microspheres uniformly. Although there is no distinct CDs found in the TEM image, the presence of N confirms the successful decoration of CDs onto WMS microspheres. Moreover, the as-prepared SPME coating based on CDs/WMS microspheres was also characterized by optical microscope (Fig. S1). Results show that the uniform coating was formed on the surface of stainless-steel wire with thickness of ∼40 μm.

Furthermore, the formation mechanism of WMS microspheres with unique morphologies was investigated based on the reported literature with modifications [35,39]. As illustrated in Fig. 2 , firstly, hydrophobic–hydrophilic CTAB formed a fibrous structure with radial morphology in the presence of cyclohexane. Then the TEOS was hydrolyzed to be negatively charged silicate molecules by urea. The generated silicate ions assembled on the radial micelle template driven by electrostatic force. Finally, the WMS microspheres were observed after removing CTAB templates. However, a small quantity of negatively charged CDs resulted in formation of smaller micelles due to the electrostatic interaction with positively charged ammonium groups of CTAB. In addition, the electrostatic repulsive force between CDs and silicate ions induced adjacent CTAB molecules to be agglomerated, thus leading to the formation of thick silica walls for CDs/WMS microsphere. Excess CDs resulted in the formation of severely agglomerative structures due to the electrostatic force among positively charged ammonium groups, negatively charged CDs, and silicate ions. Therefore, the amounts of CDs needed to be controlled for the preparation of CDs/WMS microspheres by in-situ strategy.

To demonstrate the composition structures and chemical groups of CDs/WMS microspheres, XRD and XPS analysis were performed. As shown in Fig. 3 a, a broad diffraction peak at 22.4° was detected for WMS microspheres, which indicates the presence of amorphous silica [43]. The decoration of CDs does not change the structure of WMS microspheres. XPS analysis indicated the chemical composition and functional groups of CDs/WMS microspheres (Fig. 3b–f). Fig. 3b confirms the existence of Si, O, C, and N in CDs/WMS with atomic contents of 26.68 %, 59.93 %, 12.04 %, and 1.35 %, respectively, which has been detailed in Table S2. High-resolution spectra of Si 2p (Fig. 3c) presented a distinct peak at 103.3 eV, indicating the formation of Si–O species. The high-resolution spectra of O 1s (Fig. 3d) was deconvoluted to two peaks with binding energies at 532.3 and 533.1 eV, which were attributed to C–OH/COC and adsorbed oxygen (Oε), respectively [44]. The C 1s spectra of WMS (Fig. 3e) exhibited two peaks at 284.4 and 286.6 eV corresponding to CC/CC and CO, respectively [45]. In contrast, an extra peak at 285.3 eV referring to CN species emerged in C 1s spectra of CDs/WMS owing to the introduction of CDs [46]. Moreover, the CDs/WMS presented much higher atomic content of C compared with pure WMS. N 1s spectra of CDs/WMS (Fig. 3f) was separated to three peaks at 400.9, 402.2, and 406.4 eV, which were ascribed to tertiary nitrogen atoms (N–(C3)), N–HX, and π–π stacking [47]. The elevated content of C and the presence of N further confirmed the successful doping of CDs onto the WMS microspheres.

To investigate the BET surface area and pore size distribution, N2 adsorption–desorption and pore size distribution isotherms of pure WMS, CDs/WMS-2, and CDs/WMS-3 were performed (Fig. 4 a and b). Results illustrated that the BET surface area of CDs/WMS-2 was calculated to be 650.2 m2/g, which was slightly lower than pure WMS microspheres (730.1 m2/g). The decoration of CDs onto WMS reduced the surface area of as-prepared microspheres. CDs with higher concentration further reduced the surface area to 480.6 m2/g (CDs/WMS-3) due to the effect of agglomeration, which has been confirmed by SEM characterization and preparation mechanism investigation. The pore size distribution of pure WMS and CDs/WMS-2 microspheres (Fig. 4b) presented two dominant pore diameters at 3.1 and 29.5 nm, while a distinct pore diameter at 2.6 nm was formed for CDs/WMS-3 microspheres due to the effect of severe agglomeration caused by CDs. Although the introduction of CDs reduced the BET surface area of WMS microspheres, the CDs provided abundant chemical groups and π–π bonding, which enhanced the interactions with targeted compounds.

Given that the as-prepared CDs/WMS microspheres was used as SPME coating, which undergone a thermal desorption at 250 °C in the GC–MS injector, the chemical groups of CDs/WMS before and after thermal treatment with temperature of 280 °C were investigated by FTIR (Fig. S2a). It was found that no obvious change in functional groups occurred after thermal treatment. In addition, thermally treated CDs with temperature of 280 °C still retained excellent fluorescence property, which was the typical feature of CDs (Fig. S2b). Moreover, the thermal stabilities of WMS and CDs/WMS-2 microspheres were also evaluated by TG analysis under nitrogen atmosphere (Fig. 4c). The results demonstrated that both WMS and CDs/WMS-2 microspheres exhibited excellent thermal stabilities in the temperature range of 30–800 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SPME performances and mechanisms of CDs/WMS coating
ce:section.ce:para: The extraction capacities of pure WMS and CDs/WMS microspheres for OCPs were evaluated as the SPME coatings. It can be seen from Fig. 5 a that CDs/WMS microspheres exhibit higher extraction efficiencies for eight OCPs compared with pure WMS microspheres. Notably, the extraction efficiencies present a trend of first increase and then decrease following with the increase of CDs concentration from 1 to 5 mg mL−1 in the reaction solutions, and the CDs/WMS-2 with CDs concentration of 2 mg mL−1 presents the optimum extraction capacities. Although the BET surface area of CDs/WMS reduced slightly in comparison to pure WMS microspheres, the introduction of CDs enabled the formation of π-π interaction with OCPs compounds, consequently resulting in improvement of extraction efficiencies. The decrease in extraction efficiencies of CDs/WMS-3 was ascribed to the severe agglomeration and diminution of surface area induced by excess CDs, which was illustrated in SEM and BET analysis. Therefore, the extraction performances depended on dual effects of silica and CDs. Furthermore, the comparisons of CDs/WMS coating with commercial SPME coating including PA, PDMS, and DVB/CAR/PDMS were also investigated (Fig. 5b). Results show that the CDs/WMS-2 coating displays distinctly superior extraction efficiencies compared with commercial SPME coatings.

According to the morphologies and chemical compositions characterizations of CDs/WMS microspheres, the excellent extraction performances for OCPs are ascribed to three factors, which are illustrated in Fig. 6 . Firstly, high surface area of CDs/WMS microspheres provided plentiful contact active sites for targeted compounds. Secondly, abundant chemical groups such as oxygen/nitrogen-containing groups and π bonds enhanced the interactions with OCPs molecules. Thirdly, the mesoporous structures allowed the molecules transport from outer to inner surface of microspheres, leading to improvements in adsorption rates and extraction efficiencies.

To observe the optimum extraction efficiencies for OCPs, the SPME conditions including extraction temperature, time, ionic strength of solution, and solution pH were investigated using spiked aqueous solutions with OCPs concentration of 5 ng/L. Fig. S3a displays the effect of temperature ranging from 30 to 60 °C on extraction efficiencies for eight OCPs. Results demonstrate that the extraction efficiencies increase first and then decrease due to the negative effect of excess temperature on the distribution coefficient between analytes and coating materials [48]. Therefore, 50 °C was selected as the optimum extraction temperature. Fig. S3b illustrates that the extraction efficiencies reach equilibrium within 20 min. The fast extraction time was attributed to the mesoporous structures of CDs/WMS, which provided abundant channels for molecules transport. NaCl was employed to evaluate the effect of ionic strength according to reported methods [49,50]. As shown in Fig. S3c, the presence of NaCl in aqueous solution reduces the extraction efficiencies due to the salt deposition on the coating material. Fig. S3d exhibits that no distinct changes in extraction efficiencies occur with solution pH ranging from 3 to 9, which implies that the as-prepared CDs/WMS coating can be used in a wide range of acidity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method performances for determining OCPs from water and fruit juice
ce:section.ce:para: The method performances for determining eight OCPs from water and fruit juice based on as-prepared SPME coating coupled with GC–MS were evaluated by means of linearity, limits of detection (LODs), limits of quantitation (LOQs), and relative standard deviations (RSDs). As shown in Table 1 , the proposed method exhibits excellent linearity for both water and fruit juice samples in OCPs concentration ranging from 0.2–1000 ng/L (linear coefficients (R2) ≥ 0.9551). The LODs and LOQs, which are calculated based on three and ten times of signal-to-noise, are 0.0023–0.073 ng/L and 0.0077–0.24 ng/L for water samples, respectively. The LODs and LOQs for fruit juice samples vary from 0.0062 to 0.41 ng/L and 0.02 to 1.3 ng/L. The RSDs obtained from five replicate experiments are in the ranges of 6.2 %−13.3 % and 7.3 %−13.2 % for water and fruit juice samples, respectively. To further demonstrate the superiority of proposed method, the comparisons with other SPME coatings for analysis of OCPs from water samples were investigated. As shown in Table 2 , the as-prepared CDs/WMS coating exhibited higher sensitivity compared with the SPME coatings in literatures. Moreover, the lifetime of CDs/WMS coating was also evaluated by repeated extraction and analysis of OCPs from water and fruit juice samples (Fig. S4 a and b). Results illustrated that no obvious change occurred in extraction efficiencies within 130 times for water and 100 times for fruit juice samples, implying that the as-prepared SPME coating presented excellent reusability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of OCPs from real water and fruit juice samples

ce:section.ce:para: In conclusion, CDs/WMS microspheres was successfully synthesized by in-situ stratergy and used as SPME coating material for sensitive extraction of ultra-trace OCPs from water and fruit juice. CDs/WMS composites overcame the drawbacks of CDs as sorbent to adsorb compounds from aqueous solutions. Although the modification of CDs reduced the surface area of WMS microspheres, the decorated CDs provided abundant chemical groups such as oxygen/nitrogen-containing groups and π bonds, which enhanced the extraction efficiency for OCPs. Moreover, the formed mesoporous structures on the microspheres facilitated the adsorption rate. The outstanding advantages such as high surface area, abundant chemical groups, and plentiful mesopores allowed CDs/WMS microspheres to be an excellent sorbent for SPME of ultra-trace OCPs from aqueous solutions. Finally, the proposed SPME coating based on CDs/WMS microspheres was successfully applied for determination of OCPs from real water and fruit juice samples. This study not only proposed a method for analysis of OCPs from environmental and food samples, but also explored the potential of CDs in adsorption of organic pollutants.

Shengrui Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Hailin Liu: Investigation, Visualization, Validation. Anying Long: Resources. Suling Feng: Supervision, Project administration. Chang-Po Chen: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by funding from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21976052, 21977025, 21705017), the Youth Science Foundation of Henan Normal University (20200183), and Natural Science Foundation of Guizhou Province of China ([2019]1423). We also would like to thank Gang Li from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the BET analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2022.122589.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of environmental and health effects of organochlorine pesticide residues in Africa
Chemosphere

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.M. Taiwo, A review of environmental and health effects of organochlorine pesticide residues in Africa, Chemosphere 220 (2019) 1126-1140.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A preliminary assessment of consumer’s exposure to organochlorine pesticides in fruits and vegetables and the potential health risk in Accra Metropolis, Ghana
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.K. Bempah, A. Donkor, P.O. Yeboah, B. Dubey, P. Osei-Fosu, A preliminary assessment of consumer’s exposure to organochlorine pesticides in fruits and vegetables and the potential health risk in Accra Metropolis, Ghana, Food Chem. 128 (2011) 1058-1065.
